title: How Microdosing Affects Your Relationship with Technology and Screens
body:
How Microdosing Affects Your Relationship with Technology and Screens

You’ve probably noticed how screens have quietly taken over your life. Phones, laptops, TVs - they’re everywhere. And honestly, most of us can’t imagine a day without them. But here’s the thing: microdosing, that subtle, low-dose psychedelic practice, might just change how you interact with all this tech. It’s not about dropping your devices into a bonfire (though, I get the temptation). Instead, it’s about shifting your awareness so your screen time feels less like a trap and more like a choice. Sound interesting? Let’s unpack it.

What Happens When You Microdose?

Microdosing usually means taking about 1/10th to 1/20th of a recreational psychedelic dose - say, 10 or 15 micrograms of LSD or 0.1 to 0.2 grams of dried psilocybin mushrooms. You won’t trip or hallucinate. Instead, you might notice subtle shifts: sharper focus, a slight mood lift, maybe a bit more creativity or openness. For some, it’s like a mental tune-up. For others, it’s a gentle nudge out of autopilot.

Now, imagine that slight nudge applied to how you use technology every day. How does it make you feel?

The Screen Trap: What We’re Up Against

Right?! Screens are designed to grab your attention. Notifications ping, apps pull you in, and before you know it, an hour has vanished into a black hole of social media, news, or endless scrolling. This isn’t just annoying; it affects your brain chemistry and mood. The dopamine hits from likes and messages create a loop of craving and reward, which can leave you feeling drained and disconnected.

Most of us don’t even realize how much time we spend staring at pixels. According to a 2023 study by the Scientific American, the average American spends over seven hours a day on screens. That’s a full third of your waking hours. Imagine what that does to your attention, your sleep, and your ability to be present.

Microdosing and Screen Time: The Subtle Shift

Microdosing can tweak your relationship with technology in a few key ways. It’s not magic, but it’s real. Here’s what people often report:

  • Increased awareness: You start to notice how often you’re reaching for your phone or clicking through tabs without thinking. That autopilot mode? It loosens its grip.
  • Improved focus: Instead of flitting between tasks or tabs, microdosing can help you settle into one thing at a time. That email? Finished. That article? Read with real attention.
  • Less reactivity: Notifications and pings don’t trigger the same frantic urge to check immediately. You might even enjoy the silence between alerts.
  • More mindful breaks: You’re more likely to step away from the screen for a few minutes, breathe, maybe stretch or meditate. These pauses feel refreshing, not like chores.

Here’s the thing, though: microdosing doesn’t automatically fix your screen habits. It creates space for change, but you still have to choose what to do with it.

A Moment with Lucas

Let me tell you about Lucas, a software developer I worked with recently. He was stuck in a cycle of endless Zoom meetings, Slack notifications, and 3 AM doomscrolling. He started microdosing psilocybin on a schedule - twice a week, low doses. Within two weeks, he noticed something strange: his afternoons weren’t a fog of distraction anymore. He’d close all tabs except the one he needed and actually finish his work.

Lucas told me, “I didn’t expect to feel less addicted. I thought microdosing would just make me feel ‘better.’ But it’s like I found a pause button on my compulsive scrolling. The urge to check my phone every five minutes wasn’t gone, but it felt manageable, like a gentle tap instead of a shove.”

That pause button is what many people find so useful. It’s not about cutting screens out completely. It’s about shifting from being controlled by your devices to choosing how and when you engage.

Why Does Microdosing Change How You Use Screens?

Look, the science is still catching up. But here’s what we do know. Psychedelics, even in microdoses, seem to affect the brain’s default mode network (DMN), which is linked to self-referential thoughts and mind-wandering. When the DMN quiets down, you get less stuck in loops of rumination or distraction.

This change can translate into the way you use technology. Instead of mindlessly scrolling or refreshing social feeds, microdosing nudges you toward more intentional, present use. You become less captive to dopamine-driven urges and more tuned into what really matters at the moment.

And if you want to geek out on the brain science behind it, check out this resource from Johns Hopkins Medicine. They’re leading the charge on psychedelic research, and their findings make a lot of sense when you think about your screen habits.

Microdosing and Mental Health: The Screen Connection

One reason microdosing shifts your tech use is its effect on mood and anxiety. Screens can sometimes amplify anxiety, especially social media. You know that pit in your stomach after doomscrolling or getting caught in online arguments. Microdosing can reduce that background anxiety enough that those moments don’t feel like emotional landmines.

Here’s a story from Ayelet Waldman’s A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link). She talks about how microdosing helped her manage mood swings, which in turn made her online interactions less stressful. When you’re not on edge, you’re less likely to get sucked into toxic digital drama.

And when that anxiety lifts, you might find it easier to put your phone down, to switch off emails after work, or to resist the pull of endless notifications. It’s like the screen no longer feels like an enemy or a crutch.

Practical Tips: Combining Microdosing with Tech Boundaries

Microdosing can open the door, but you still need to walk through it. Here are some ideas to make the most of your new awareness:

  • Set clear tech limits: Try scheduled “phone-free” times during your day. Even 30 minutes can reset your brain. Use built-in screen timers or apps to help.
  • Create a mindful workspace: Keep distractions out. Close unnecessary tabs. Turn off non-essential notifications.
  • Use tools that promote calm: For example, a meditation zafu cushion can help you build a daily meditation habit (a meditation zafu cushion (paid link)). Sitting still for a few minutes can reduce the urge to check your phone.
  • Take sensory breaks: Wrap yourself in a soft therapy blanket (a soft therapy blanket (paid link)) and just breathe. These breaks help calm your nervous system.
  • Read offline: Swap an hour of scrolling for a fascinating book. Merlin Sheldrake’s Entangled Life by Merlin Sheldrake (paid link) is a great choice if you want to reconnect with nature’s wonders instead of pixels.

Potential Pitfalls: Be Honest with Yourself

Look, microdosing isn’t a cure-all. Some people find that it actually makes them more sensitive to screen overload because they notice it more clearly. Others might get caught up in new digital obsessions, like tracking their microdoses obsessively or joining online forums that fuel anxiety.

Microdosing can also create a false sense of “I’m fixed now,” which can make you ignore deeper issues around tech use, like procrastination or avoidance. If you find yourself just swapping one habit for another, that’s okay. It’s part of the process. The key is to be curious about what’s really driving your screen time.

How to Track Your Progress

Try keeping a simple journal for a couple of weeks when you start microdosing. Note your screen time, mood, focus, and any urges to check your devices. You don’t need to be perfect - just honest. Over time, you’ll get a clearer picture of what’s shifting.

That’s how Lucas figured out his afternoons were finally “sacred” again. And it might be how you reclaim your time, too.

Where to Learn More

If you want to explore this topic further, check out my article How to Microdose Safely. It dives into practical steps for getting started, which can help you integrate microdosing with intentional tech use.

And, of course, if you want to connect directly or explore more about consciousness and healing, visit kalesh.love. There’s a community here ready to support you.

Final Thoughts

Truth is, microdosing won’t magically make your phone disappear or your laptop stop buzzing. What it can do is give you a little breathing room in your mind, a chance to notice how you’re spending your time and energy. When you combine that with simple, consistent habits, you might just find yourself scrolling less and living more.

How does that make you feel? Ready to try it yourself? Remember, this is a journey. You don’t have to do it perfectly. Just start noticing. Just start choosing. The rest will follow.
